source_metadata.title_raw: Amy Webber - A-19-010 - February 28, 2019 - Long Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 28, 2019

Amy Webber
Deputy City Attorney
Long Beach City Attorney's Office
333 West Ocean Blvd., 11th Floor
Long Beach, CA 90802-4664

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-19-010

Dear Ms. Webber:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Long Beach
Councilmember Stacy Mungo regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

        Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

        Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTIONS

        May Councilmember Mungo participate in a decision regarding a tenant protection
ordinance:

        1. If she works as a consultant to the nonprofit Apartment Association,
California Southern Cities ("Association"), which serves the area rental housing
industry?

        2. During her negotiations or coming to an arrangement period regarding
this work?

_____________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-010
Page No. 2

3. Due to her spouse's ownership of a triplex rental property in the City of
Long Beach?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. If she accepts the position, the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on her source of income interest in the Association.

2. No. It is foreseeable that the Association, the prospective employer, will receive a
measurable financial benefit or loss as a result of the decision. She may not participate in a decision
relating to the Association if she is negotiating or has any arrangement concerning employment.

3. Yes. Based on the general information provided as to the decision's scope, its effect on
Councilmember Mungo's rental property financial interest will be indistinguishable from its effect
on the public generally. She is not prohibited from participation in the decision due to this financial
interest. We recommend she seek additional advice as needed, once the decision is more defined.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Councilmember Mungo is serving her second term as a member of the Long Beach City
Council. The nine-member City Council has broad authority over contracts and policies in the City,
and over the provisions of the Long Beach Municipal Code.

Councilmember Mungo has been employed by the County of Los Angeles, and is currently
on maternity leave. She was recently offered a position as consultant to the Apartment Association,
California Southern Cities ("Association"). This is a nonprofit association composed of more than
2,500 members in an area including more than 50 cities, including Long Beach, ranging from Los
Angeles/Orange County border north to Burbank. You provided a link to the Association's web
site, which indicates its members include property owners, management companies and vendors to
the rental housing industry. The Association offers "cost-effective services for owners; mobilizes
influential legislative advocacy, provides immense discounts and buying power; presents quality
professional education and serves as the springboard for powerful industry-wide coalitions."2 In
additional information provided by email, you state that the Association does not track where the
members own property or "the make-up of their assets" (types of rental properties owned), and that
many owners own property in multiple cities.

The Association was recently advised by its national affiliate, the National
Association of Apartment Owners, to improve certain critical services, including technology
services, marketing, personnel and membership. Due to her experience as a turnaround
agent during her time at LA County, as the Association's consultant Ms. Mungo would address
improvements to these issues on a six-month to one-year contract basis. Legislative and
policy advocacy would be specifically excluded from her scope of work.

2AACSC Apartment Association California Southern Cities http://www.apt-assoc.com/ viewed January 30,
2019.

File No. A-19-010
Page No. 3

In addition, Ms. Mungo's spouse owns a tri-plex (i.e., three units) rental property in the
City. The tri-plex property is held as his separate property, although it is not subject to a pre-nuptial
agreement. Ms. Mungo does not participate in the management of the property, and is not
currently a member of the Association.

In the coming weeks, the City Council is scheduled to consider an item to amend the Long
Beach Municipal Code to add code-required tenant protections. The tenant protections involve
property owner obligations, and is an item which the Association has opposed in the past. The
proposed tenant protections ordinance has not been drafted as of yet.³ It is anticipated it will involve
tenant protections for business-licensed residential properties of four or more units, would require
landlords to pay for tenant relocation, limit evictions to "just cause" and limit the amount rents
could be increased. The ordinance may also address rental assistance and relocation for senior
citizens, and added means to address issues of negligent landlords, neighborhood blight and code
enforcement issues. While City staff reported they would not recommend a policy for properties of
less than four units, you state that the Council has no restrictions on the number of units it could
regulate.

Per the City's Housing Element, there are 176,188 housing units in the City. That includes
single-family, duplex, triplex, multi-family (defined as four units or more), and condominium
units. Based on data included in the Housing Element, 42% of the City's housing units are owner-
occupied, and 58% of the housing units are renter-occupied. The City does not regulate the single-
family, duplex, triplex, and condominium units that are being rented. A business license is required
for multi-family rental properties of four or more units, and there are 70,317 multi-family rental
units in the City. 70,317 is approximately 40% of the total housing units in the City (176,7188), but
that number does not capture other housing types that are being rented. It is unknown the
percentage of Association members that are Long Beach property owners that may be impacted by
the ordinance.

You request whether Councilmember Mungo's interests prohibit her participation in
decisions involving tenants and landlords as a general matter. This analysis focuses on the specific
tenant protection ordinance decision at issue, and we advise that she request additional advice, as
needed, based on the particular facts involved in a future decision.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interests
implicated by these facts are:

³ The council has not directed staff to draft an ordinance. The council has only requested staff bring back
practices employed in other cities. None of the cities reviewed by staff called out properties of 3 units or less.

File No. A-19-010
Page No. 4

• A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• A real property interest, where an official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.)

If Councilmember Mungo agrees to work as a consultant on a contract basis with the Association, she will have a source of income interest in the Association for income promised or received in regards to her contract work. She will also have a business entity and source of income interest in her consultant business as a contract consultant, once the above thresholds are met.

Where her spouse owns the rental property as his sole separate property, Councilmember Mungo has a business entity, real property and source of income interest in the tri-plex rental property. An "indirect investment or interest" includes any investment or interest owned by the spouse of a public official. (Section 87103.) An "interest in a source of income" includes a pro rata share of the income of any business entity in which the official or spouse owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).) She also has a source of income interest in her spouse's rental tenants to the extent the rental business has received $500 or more from the tenant in the twelve months prior to a decision.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision at issue. The standard for materiality depends upon whether the interest is explicitly involved, as well as the type of financial interest. A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the official's financial interest it is explicitly involved as a named party in, or subject of, the decision.⁴ (Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

⁴ A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

File No. A-19-010
Page No. 5

The Association as a Source of Income

The proposal for tenant protections does not explicitly involve the Association. Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) provides the standard for materiality of a nonprofit source of income. Regulation states materiality is found if:

(3) The source is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss, or the official knows or has reason to know that the nonprofit has an interest in real property that will be financially affected under the standards applied to a financial interest in Regulation 18702.2;

The Association has opposed similar ordinances in the past on behalf of its membership, and markets itself as a source of industry-wide coalition and legislative advocacy for its members. You state it is not known the percentage of Association members potentially impacted by the decision. However, it is likely that the Association's role in opposing the decision will raise its public profile, create publicity, and impact its membership efforts. This will result in a measurable benefit or loss to the Association.

Additionally, Regulation 18702.3(c), the "nexus" rule, states the following materiality standard:

Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.

Under the "nexus" standard, there is a presumption the private employer benefits from the actions of the employee in her official capacity, when an employee earns income to accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what she does as an official. (See for example Torres Advice Letter, No. A-17-117.) In this case, Councilmember Mungo would be hired to address the Association's critical services, including membership and marketing. As noted above, membership and marketing for the Association will be aided or hindered by the decision. Accordingly, the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on her source of income interest in the Association. Due to this finding, no further analysis of her anticipated consultant business is necessary.

Prospective Employment with the Association

The ban on influencing prospective employment prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision that directly relates to a prospective employer while negotiating, or after reaching, an employment arrangement. (Section 87407; Regulation 18747.) This law expands the Act's conflict-of-interest rules and related disqualification obligations to situations where a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the prospective employer even though the official does not yet have an economic interest in the employer.

File No. A-19-010
Page No. 6

Councilmember Mungo is currently in negotiations with the Association as a prospective
source of employment. Section 87407 prohibits a public official from making, participating in or
using her official position to influence any governmental decision "directly relating" to any person
with whom she is negotiating or has any arrangement concerning prospective employment.
Regulation 18747(b)(2)(B) states that a decision directly relates to prospective nonprofit entity
employment if it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect is material under the standard for
a source of income interest in Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) or (3). As noted above, the standard in
Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), is met under the facts. Therefore, the decision is directly related to her
prospective employment and she may not participate in the decision during the period in which the
Association is a prospective employer. A public official is not "negotiating" or does not have an
"arrangement" concerning prospective employment if he or she rejects or is rejected for
employment. (Regulation 18747(c)(3).)

Spouse's Tri-Plex Rental Business Interest

Based on the facts provided, this analysis assumes the tenant protection ordinance will
impact all residential rental units in the City. Because the ordinance would be applicable on a city-
wide basis, the decision would not explicitly involve her spouse's rental business as a subject of the
decision.⁵ Looking to the applicable materiality standard in Regulation 18702.1(b), a financial
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a
privately-held business entity.⁶ This includes decisions that:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity
in which the business entity is engaged;

It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision, by imposing tenant protections and property
owner obligations on rental properties in the City, would contribute to a change in the value of her
spouse's rental business. A tenant protection ordinance regulates the rental market conditions and
may restrict the income producing potential of rental businesses in the City. It is reasonably
foreseeable that this decision will have a material financial effect on the Councilmember's residential
rental business.⁷

_____________
⁵ See Gelb Advice Letter A-91-523, and Condotti Advice Letter A-17-288.

⁶ Under Regulation 18702.3, the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is the
same as for an interest in the business entity itself.

⁷ We note that the official also has a source of income interest in her spouse's tenants, provided the tenant has
paid rent aggregating to $500 in the 12 months before the decision. However, it is unnecessary to consider the financial
interests in the tenants in light of the above materiality findings. She also has a real property interest in the tri-plex
property; however, that interest is not implicated by these facts.

File No. A-19-010
Page No. 7

Public Generally Exception

A public official may participate if the decision's effect on her financial interests affects a
significant segment of the public and is not unique to her interests. Regulation 18703(b) states a
significant segment of the public is at least 25 percent of:

(1) All businesses or non-profit entities within the official's jurisdiction;

(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within
the official's jurisdiction; or

(3) All individuals within the official's jurisdiction.

This decision will impact a significant segment of the City. If the decision applies to all
residential rental units in the City, it will impact 58 percent of the total residential rental units. If it
applies to four-unit and greater rentals, it will impact 40 percent of the total residential rental units.

However, the public generally exception does not apply if there is a "unique effect" on a
public official's financial interest, which includes a disproportionate effect on:

(1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity.

(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity of a
project that is the subject of a decision.

(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the
cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or properties
that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest.

(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the
official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a
decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage.

(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the
person is a source of income or gifts to the official.

(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family

(Regulation 18703(c).)

Because this decision would extend certain restrictions to all or a segment of the City's
rental market, the decision would foreseeably have a unique effect on the membership, and thereby
income, of the Association. (Regulation 18703(c)(5).) Therefore, the public generally exception is
not applicable to her interest in the Association as a source of income or as a potential source of
employment.

File No. A-19-010
Page No. 8

The public generally exception is applicable to her interest in the tri-plex rental units, only so long as none of the above factors apply to this interest. Please contact our office for further advice if necessary, as the decision comes into focus.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of 
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interests 
implicated by these facts are:

practices employed in other cities. None of the cities reviewed by staff called out properties of 3 units or less.  
• 
A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or 
more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A real property interest, where an official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033). 
• 
An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in 
value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the 
time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those 
of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
If Councilmember Mungo agrees to work as a consultant on a contract basis with the 
Association, she will have a source of income interest in the Association for income promised or 
received in regards to her contract work. She will also have a business entity and.source of income 
interest in her consultant business as a contract consultant, once the above thresholds are met.

Mungo has a business entity, real property and source of income interest in the tri-plex rental 
property. An "indirect investment or interest" includes any investment or interest owned by the 
spouse of a public official. (Section 87103.) An "interest in a source of income" includes a pro rata

or beneficially, a IO-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).) She also has a source of income 
interest in her spouse's rental tenants to the extent the rental busjness has received $500 or more 
from the tenant in the twelve months prior to a decision.

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision at issue. The standard for materiality depends upon whether the interest is 
explicitly involved, as well as the type of financial interest. A financial effect is presumed 
reasonably foreseeable where the official's financial interest it is explicitly involved as a named 
party in, or subject of, the decision. 4 (Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest that is not 
explicitly involved in the decision, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
4 A financial interest is the subject ofa proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the 
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2( a)( I)-( 6). 


The Association as a Source of Income  
The proposal for tenant protections does not explicitly involve the Association. Regulation 
18702.3(a)(3) provides the standard for materiality of a nonprofit source of income. Regulation 
states materiality is found if: 
(3) The source is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit or 
loss, or the official knows or has reason to know that the nonprofit has an interest 
in real property that will be financially affected under the standards applied to a 
financial interest in Regulation 18702.2; 
The Association has opposed similar ordinances in the past on behalf of its membership, and 
markets itself as a source of industry-wide coalition and legislative advocacy for its members. You 
state it is not known the percentage of Association members potentially impacted by the decision. 
However, it is likely that the Association's role in opposing the decision will raise its public profile, 
create publicity, and impact its membership efforts. This will result in a measurable benefit or loss

income to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or is

defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.

Prospective Employment with the AssociaUon 
The ban on influencing prospective employment prohibits any public official from making, 
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision that directly relates to a prospective 
employer while negotiating, or after reaching, an employment arrangement. (Section 87407; 
Regulation 18747.) This law expands the Act's conflict-of-interest rules and related disqualification 
obligations to situations where a decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on the prospective employer even though the official does not yet have an economic interest 
in the employer.  
Councilmember Mungo is currently in negotiations with the Association as a prospective 
source of employment. Section 87407 prohibits a public official from making, participating in or 
using her official positon to influence any governmental decision "directly relating" to any person 
with whom she is negotiating or has any arrangement concerning prospective employment. 
Regulation 18747(b)(2)(B) states that a decision directly relates to prospective nonprofit entity 
employment if it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect is material under the standard for 
a source of income interest in Regulation 18702.3(a)(l) or (3). As noted above, the standard in

Spouse's Tri-Plex Rental Business Interest 
Based on the facts provided, this analysis assumes the tenant protection ordinance will 
impact all residential rental units in the City. Because the ordinance would be applicable on a city-
wide basis, the decision would not explicitly involve her spouse's rental business as a subject of the 
decision. 5 Looking to the applicable materiality standard in Regulation 18702.1 (b ), a financial 
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a 
privately-held business entity.6 This includes decisions that: 
(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity 
in which the business entity is engaged; 
It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision, by imposing tenant protections and property 
owner obligations on rental properties in the City, would contribute to a change in the value of her 
spouse's rental business. A tenant protection ordinance regulates the rental market conditions and 
may restrict the income producing potential ofrental businesses in the City. It is reasonably 
foreseeable that this decision will have a material finaical effect on the Councilmember's residential 
rental business. 7 
5 See Gelb Advice Letter A-91-523, and Condotti Advice Letter A-17-288. 
6 Ur:ider Regulation 18702.3, the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is the 
same as for an interest in the business entity itself. 
7 We note that the official also has a source of income interest in her spouse's tenants, provided the tenant has 
paid rent aggregating to $500 in the 12 months before the decision. However, it is unnecessary to consider the financial 
interests in the tenants in light of the above materiality findings. She also has a real property interest in the tri-plex 
property; however, that interest is not implicated by these facts. 


Public Generally Exception  
A public official may participate if the decisitm's effect on her financial interests affects a 
significant segment of the public and is not unique to her interests. Regulation 18703(b) states a 
significant segment of the public is at least 25 percent of: 
( 1) All businesses or non-profit entities within the official's jurisdiction; 
(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within 
the official's jurisdiction; or 
(3) All individuals within the official's jurisdiction. 
This decision will impact a significant segment of the City. If the decision applies to all 
residential rental units in the City, it will impact 58 percent of the total residential rental units. If it 
applies to four-unit and greater rentals, it will impact 40 percent of the total residential rental units. 
However, the public generally exception does not apply ifthere is a "unique effect" on a 
public official's financial interest, which includes a disproportionate effect on: 
(1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the 
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity of a 
project that is the subject of a decision. 
(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the 
cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or properties 
that_ is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the 
official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a 
decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the 
person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family 
(Regulation 18703(c).) 
Because this decision would extend certain restrictions to all or a segment of the City's 
rental market, the decision would foreseeably have a unique effect on the membership, and thereby 
income, of the Association. (Regulation 18703(c)(5).) Therefore, the public generally exception is 
not applicable to her interest in the Association as a source of income or as a potential source of 
employment.  
The public generally exception is applicable to her interest in the tri-plex rental units, only 
so long as none of the above factors apply to this interest. Please contact our office for further 
advice if necessary, as the decision comes into focus.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Mungo participate in a decision regarding a tenant protection 
ordinance: 
1. If she works as a consultant to the nonprofit Apartment Association, 
California Southern Cities ("Association"), which serves the area rental housing 
industry? 
2. During her negotiations or coming to an arrangement period regarding 
this work?FileNo. A-19-010  
3. Due to her spouse's ownership of a triplex rental property in the City of 
Long Beach?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. If she accepts the position, the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and 
material financial effect on her source of income interest in the Association. 
2. No. It is foreseeable that the Association, the prospective employer, will receive a 
measurable financial benefit or loss as a result of the decision. She may not participate in a decision 
relating to the Association if she is negotiating or has any arrangement concerning employment. 
3. Yes. Based on the general information provided as to the decision's scope, its effect on 
Councilmember Mungo' s rental property financial interest will be indistinguishable from its effect 
on the public generally. She is not prohibited from participation in the decision due to this financial 
interest. We recommend she seek additional advice as needed, once the decision is more defined.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.
